content.text: What it is

Service Level Indicators for LLM API integration services are the specific, measurable signals that a team agrees to use as the authoritative measure of whether the service is delivering value to its users. An SLI is not a metric -- every monitoring system has hundreds of metrics. An SLI is a metric that has been elevated to represent the reliability contract: if this number is bad, the service is failing its users.

Selecting the right SLIs for agentic AI services requires confronting a challenge that does not exist for simple request-response APIs: the output of an LLM is probabilistic and context-dependent. A service can have 100% availability and sub-second latency while producing outputs that are wrong, hallucinated, or harmful. For agentic systems that take consequential actions, this means availability and latency SLIs alone are insufficient -- quality and safety SLIs must be part of the reliability contract.

The SLI selection process is deliberate and bounded. Google's SRE practice recommends defining no more than five SLIs per service, because each SLI requires an SLO, an error budget, and an alert. More than five SLIs creates an operational burden that dilutes attention during incidents. The discipline of choosing the most important five forces the team to think clearly about what 'reliable' actually means for their service.
Why it matters

Wrong SLI selection is one of the most consequential mistakes in SRE practice because it means the team will be optimizing for the wrong thing for months or years. A team that measures 'HTTP 200 response rate' for an agentic AI service may consistently meet their SLO while their users experience high rates of incorrect, incomplete, or harmful agent outputs that never produce a non-200 response.

For LLM API integration specifically, the upstream provider's availability is not the same as the customer-visible service availability. If the LLM provider is 99.99% available but the team's prompt engineering causes 15% of responses to fail validation and require costly retries, the customer-visible task completion rate is far below 99.99%. The SLI must measure what the customer experiences, not what the infrastructure reports.

Correct SLI selection also drives engineering priorities. A team whose primary SLI is task completion rate will invest in retry logic, fallback models, and graceful degradation. A team whose primary SLI is raw LLM API availability will invest in uptime monitoring. These are very different engineering decisions with very different customer outcomes.
Benefits
Focuses team attention on what actually matters to users: task outcomes rather than infrastructure metrics.
Surfaces hidden failure modes: quality and safety SLIs catch degradations that availability and latency SLIs completely miss.
Grounds SLO conversations in user experience: when stakeholders ask 'how reliable is the agent?', the answer is a user-meaningful number like 'tasks complete successfully 99.4% of the time'.
Enables error budget allocation: only well-defined SLIs can support error budget calculations that guide release decisions.
Reduces alert noise: teams with too many metrics and no SLI discipline get overwhelmed by noise; SLIs identify which metrics deserve pages.
Trade-offs

Quality SLIs are expensive to build: measuring hallucination rate or output accuracy at production scale requires either human review (not scalable) or an automated evaluation model (additional cost and complexity).
A small SLI set may miss important failure modes: choosing only five SLIs means some degradation patterns will not trigger SLO breaches, even if they affect users.
SLI definition drift: teams often start with the right SLIs but gradually let them drift as the service changes, without updating the SLI definitions to match new behavior.
Measurement lag for quality SLIs: availability and latency can be measured in real time; output quality often requires asynchronous evaluation pipelines with delays of minutes to hours.
How it works

SLI selection follows the 'good events / total events' ratio pattern: identify events that represent user interactions, define what makes an event 'good' (successful, fast enough, high enough quality, safe), and measure the fraction of events that meet that standard. For an agentic AI service, 'events' are typically agent tasks, not HTTP requests.

The selection process starts by mapping the user journey through the service. For a document processing agent: user submits document, agent reads and analyzes, agent calls tools, agent synthesizes output, user receives result. Each stage can fail. The primary SLI should cover the end-to-end user outcome -- did the user receive a correct result within an acceptable time? -- rather than any individual stage.

Secondary SLIs cover the sub-components that most commonly cause end-to-end failure: LLM API availability, tool call success rate, and latency at each stage. These provide diagnostic resolution during incidents: if the primary SLI degrades, the secondary SLIs identify which component caused the degradation.

Safety SLIs are special: they are not measured as ratios of 'good events' but as counts of 'bad events' -- guardrail violations, PII exposures, injection attempts that bypassed filters. These SLIs have a target of zero or near-zero, and any non-zero reading triggers investigation regardless of whether an SLO breach has occurred.
Architecture

attrs.alt: SLI taxonomy and selection decision matrix for agentic AI services

content.text: The five SLI categories for agentic AI services are availability, latency, quality, throughput, and safety. The decision matrix maps service patterns to appropriate primary and secondary SLIs. Task-completion-rate is the recommended primary availability SLI for most agent services because it measures the outcome the user cares about.
When to use it

SLI selection happens once per service, at initial SLO definition time, and revisits happen when the service's behavior changes significantly (new model, new tool integrations, new user-facing features that change the task definition). Do not select SLIs after launch under pressure -- the rushed selection will not reflect what users actually care about.

Re-evaluate SLIs when: (1) the primary SLI is consistently met but user satisfaction scores are declining (signal that the SLI is measuring the wrong thing), (2) the team cannot identify the root cause of a primary SLI degradation because secondary SLIs are insufficient, or (3) the service adds a new category of risk (e.g., the system starts taking real-world actions like sending emails, requiring a new safety SLI).
How to implement
Map the user journey end-to-end: what does a user submit, what does the agent do, and what does the user receive? Each stage is a potential SLI measurement point.
Identify the single most important outcome for the user (the primary SLI). For most agent services, this is task completion rate or task success rate.
Define 'good' precisely for each candidate SLI: a task is 'good' if it completes successfully (no error, no timeout, output passes validation) within the defined latency threshold.

Add latency SLIs as ratios: instead of measuring raw P95 latency, measure the fraction of tasks completing within a threshold (e.g., 95% of tasks complete within 120 seconds). This makes them error-budget compatible.
For LLM-specific services, add a Time to First Token (TTFT) SLI for interactive use cases and an LLM API success rate SLI to distinguish provider-caused failures from self-caused failures.
Define at least one quality SLI, even if it uses sampling or human review: agree with stakeholders that 'reliable' requires correct outputs, not just completed requests.
Define safety SLIs as count-based (not ratio-based): target zero prompt injection bypasses, zero unauthorized data exposures per month.

Instrument everything before launch: SLIs require counters and histograms in the application layer, not just infrastructure metrics. Add spans to every tool call, every LLM API request, and every output validation step.
Examples
Python instrumentation for collecting SLI signals in an agentic AI task processor:

from typing import Dict, List
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class SLIDefinition:
    name: str
    category: str        # availability, latency, quality, throughput, safety
    numerator_query: str # PromQL for good events
    denominator_query: str # PromQL for all events
    unit: str
    slo_window: str

# SLI definitions for a long-running document-processing agent
DOC_AGENT_SLIS: List[SLIDefinition] = [
    SLIDefinition(
        name="task_completion_rate",
        category="availability",
        numerator_query='sum(rate(agent_tasks_total{status="completed"}[{window}]))',
        denominator_query='sum(rate(agent_tasks_total[{window}]))',
        unit="ratio",
        slo_window="30d",
    ),
    SLIDefinition(
        name="task_latency_p95_good",
        category="latency",
        numerator_query=(
            'sum(rate(agent_task_duration_seconds_bucket{le="120"}[{window}]))'
        ),
        denominator_query='sum(rate(agent_task_duration_seconds_count[{window}]))',
        unit="ratio of tasks completing within 120s",
        slo_window="30d",
    ),
    SLIDefinition(
        name="llm_call_availability",
        category="availability",
        numerator_query='sum(rate(llm_api_calls_total{status!="error"}[{window}]))',
        denominator_query='sum(rate(llm_api_calls_total[{window}]))',
        unit="ratio",
        slo_window="30d",
    ),
    SLIDefinition(
        name="guardrail_coverage",
        category="safety",
        numerator_query='sum(rate(agent_outputs_guardrail_checked_total[{window}]))',
        denominator_query='sum(rate(agent_outputs_total[{window}]))',
        unit="ratio of outputs checked",
        slo_window="30d",
    ),
]

def format_sli_query(sli: SLIDefinition, window: str = "5m") -> str:
    """Format SLI PromQL queries with a specific window."""
    num = sli.numerator_query.replace("{window}", window)
    denom = sli.denominator_query.replace("{window}", window)
    return f"({num}) / ({denom})"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    for sli in DOC_AGENT_SLIS:
        print(f"SLI: {sli.name} ({sli.category})")
        print(f"  Query: {format_sli_query(sli)}")
        print()
Grafana dashboard panel definitions for the SLI suite:

# grafana/sli_dashboard.yaml
# SLI panel definitions for agentic AI service
panels:
  - title: "Task Completion Rate (Primary Availability SLI)"
    type: stat
    targets:
      - expr: |
          sum(rate(agent_tasks_total{status="completed",service="$service"}[5m]))
          / sum(rate(agent_tasks_total{service="$service"}[5m]))
        legendFormat: "completion_rate"
    thresholds:
      - color: red
        value: 0
      - color: yellow
        value: 0.99   # Below SLO target
      - color: green
        value: 0.999  # At or above SLO target

  - title: "Task Duration P95 (Latency SLI)"
    type: graph
    targets:
      - expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.95,
            sum(rate(agent_task_duration_seconds_bucket{service="$service"}[5m]))
            by (le)
          )
        legendFormat: "p95 duration"
      - expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.50,
            sum(rate(agent_task_duration_seconds_bucket{service="$service"}[5m]))
            by (le)
          )
        legendFormat: "p50 duration"

  - title: "LLM API Call Success Rate"
    type: stat
    targets:
      - expr: |
          sum(rate(llm_api_calls_total{status!="error",service="$service"}[5m]))
          / sum(rate(llm_api_calls_total{service="$service"}[5m]))

  - title: "Guardrail Coverage (Safety SLI)"
    type: stat
    targets:
      - expr: |
          sum(rate(agent_outputs_guardrail_checked_total{service="$service"}[5m]))
          / sum(rate(agent_outputs_total{service="$service"}[5m]))
    thresholds:
      - color: red
        value: 0
      - color: red
        value: 0.999  # Any gap in guardrail coverage is critical
      - color: green
        value: 1.0
Real-world example

A legal research firm deployed an agentic AI citation-checking service. Initially, the team selected API availability (HTTP success rate against the LLM provider) as their primary SLI. They consistently achieved 99.9% on this metric for the first three months. However, product feedback revealed that approximately 8% of citations generated by the agent were incorrect or hallucinated -- a quality failure the availability SLI completely masked.

The team added a quality SLI: citation accuracy rate, measured by sampling 5% of outputs and running them through an automated citation validator. The new SLI revealed the accuracy problem had existed since launch. The team set an SLO of 98% citation accuracy over 30 days and the error budget immediately showed they were in deficit. This drove two model prompt engineering improvements that brought accuracy to 99.2% within six weeks.
The lesson: the right SLIs surface hidden problems. The wrong SLIs let teams declare reliability while users experience failure.
Common mistakes
Using infrastructure SLIs (LLM API availability) as the primary user-facing SLI: the provider can be 100% up while the agent produces garbage outputs.
Measuring only success/failure without measuring quality: an agent that always returns a response with no errors is not reliable if those responses are wrong.
Too many SLIs: more than five SLIs creates alerting complexity that slows incident response and dilutes error budget thinking.

SLI definition that excludes retries: if the system retries failed LLM calls transparently, the SLI should measure the final outcome from the user's perspective, not the raw first-attempt success rate.

Not accounting for task duration in availability SLIs: a 10-minute task that fails at minute 9 should count as a much more significant 'bad event' than a 1-second request that fails, but naive counting treats them equally.
Skipping safety SLIs: teams that treat safety as out-of-scope for SLI/SLO practice lose the ability to track and hold accountability for safety degradations over time.
Best practices
Always make task completion rate or task success rate the primary SLI for agent services -- it is the metric that directly represents user value.
Define SLIs in the user's language, not the system's: 'fraction of user tasks that complete successfully' not 'fraction of LLM API calls that return 200'.

Separate provider SLIs from product SLIs: track LLM provider availability and latency separately from customer-visible task completion rate so you can distinguish provider-caused from self-caused SLO breaches.
For latency SLIs, use histogram_quantile in Prometheus or equivalent, not average latency: averages hide the long tail that users actually experience.
Build quality SLI measurement in from day one even if sampling only 1% of outputs: it is much harder to add quality instrumentation after launch than to include it from the start.
Review SLI definitions quarterly against user feedback and post-incident data: the best SLI set reflects the most common ways users experience the service as unreliable.
Performance and scalability

SLI measurement adds overhead to every agent task. For high-throughput services processing thousands of tasks per minute, the instrumentation cost can be non-trivial. Use sampling strategies for expensive measurements: quality SLIs measured on 5-10% of outputs rather than 100% provide statistically meaningful data at much lower cost. For latency SLIs, Prometheus histograms with appropriate bucket boundaries add only microseconds of overhead per measurement.

Recording rules in Prometheus pre-compute SLI ratios over common windows (5m, 1h, 6h) as background processes, so real-time dashboard queries and alert evaluations are cheap aggregations over pre-computed values rather than expensive instant computations.
Tooling and ecosystem
Prometheus: primary SLI data collection via counters and histograms. Use recording rules to pre-compute SLI ratios at multiple window sizes.
Grafana: SLI dashboards with threshold-based coloring tied to SLO targets. The Grafana SLO plugin visualizes error budget consumption directly.
Datadog: SLO tracking built in; supports metric-based and monitor-based SLOs that can be mapped to task completion rates.
OpenTelemetry: standardized instrumentation library for emitting SLI signals from any language runtime, including tracing spans that can be aggregated into SLI ratios.
Sloth or pyrra: open-source tools that generate Prometheus recording rules and alert rules from declarative SLO definitions, reducing boilerplate.
LLM evaluation frameworks (RAGAS, DeepEval, Phoenix): automated quality measurement pipelines that can feed data into quality SLIs.
Comparison with alternatives

SLI/SLO vs. uptime percentage: traditional '99.9% uptime' targets use crude availability definitions that include maintenance windows and exclude partial degradations. SLI/SLO models measure what users experience continuously, making them more honest and more actionable.

SLI/SLO vs. APDEX score: APDEX combines latency and availability into a single score. It is easier to communicate to business stakeholders but loses the diagnostic resolution of separate SLIs that identify which dimension is degrading during an incident.

Synthetic monitoring vs. real user monitoring: some teams measure SLIs using synthetic test tasks rather than real user traffic. Synthetic monitoring provides consistent measurement but may miss failure modes that only appear at production traffic patterns and load.
Security and reliability

SLI data itself must be protected. If SLI dashboards reveal task completion rates and failure patterns, they may expose information about which user segments are experiencing degradation -- which could be used to infer business metrics or customer data. Access-control SLI dashboards to engineering and SRE teams, not exposed publicly.

Safety SLIs require particular care: if the guardrail trigger rate SLI shows a spike, that spike may indicate an ongoing attack (prompt injection campaign). Safety SLI anomalies should trigger immediate security review, not just SRE response.
Mental model

Think of SLI selection as choosing which instruments to put on the cockpit dashboard. A 747 has thousands of sensors, but the pilot's primary flight display shows only six or seven key indicators because those are the ones whose deviation from nominal requires immediate action. The other sensors are available for diagnosis but do not demand pilot attention at cruise.

The primary SLI is the altimeter: it tells you whether you are at the right altitude (the service is delivering value). Secondary SLIs are the engine instruments: they tell you why the altitude is wrong if the altimeter moves. Safety SLIs are the fire warning lights: they are never supposed to illuminate, and if they do, everything else stops.
Key takeaways
For agentic AI services, task completion rate (not LLM API availability) should be the primary SLI because it measures what users actually experience.
Quality and safety SLIs are required for AI services; availability and latency alone are insufficient reliability measures for systems that produce probabilistic outputs.
Limit SLI count to five or fewer per service to maintain operational clarity and incident response speed.
Define SLIs as 'good events / total events' ratios to make them error-budget compatible.
Instrument SLI measurement from day one, including quality sampling pipelines; retrofitting quality measurement after launch is difficult and costly.
